content:
"the arena floor" is a 3lb meltybrain bot. The first iteration competed at NHRL in October. It lost both its fights. 
This aluminum body did not handle impacts well.

The software it runs is Melontype, open source meltybrain I initially wrote using ChatGPT.   https://github.com/zenith-parsec/melontype/

Accelerometer: H3LIS331DL - the Adafruit boards cost much more than the Sparkfun boards for this chip. Source currently supports Adafruit. I think I just need to change I2C address for Sparkfun board.
ESCs:  https://repeat-robotics.com/buy/am32/ x 2 
Motors:  D3530 1450kv (amazon ones. approx 2808 stator?) x 2
Wheels: Banebots T81 2-7/8" x 0.8" (Blue flavor softness. 50, i think)
Processor: Teensy 4.0

```c++
// read the line above this again, and if you keep going, remember that it's all your fault now.
```

I feel this. 😅
Truly a meltybrain mood.

the main problem (apart from strength) with the "wheels on a box" design was that g-forces keep pulling the wheels off. 
 - they're quite heavy wheels (80g for the wheel, hubs and clip)
 - the wheels are facing outwards, so when you go faster they want to go away from you.

first partial redesign to solve these problems:
 - change to 2" T81 wheels which are much lighter.
 - face the wheels inwards.

Still the same general box shape. change materials to AR500 ring, UHMW frame with CF top and bottom. (1/8", 1/2", 2mm in this weird design.)

iterated. 
make it as narrow as the wheels allow while keeping length.  (this would probably be unstable around the short axis during acceleration due to the small moment on that axis. or it could be "sporty". )
more sensible AR500/UHMW/CF ratios of 3/8" /  1/2" /  2mm.

And the most boring one is the current design. It's like there are shapes that certain things get drawn towards...
It's thinner, at 3/8" /  3/8" / 2mm  and still needs some optimizing.

I'm feeling this will be the next body for "the arena floor".

also, the weapon is part of the body now. (major change from the + shaped one)
Something that might save you some volume is wrapping the wheels around the motors, rather than sitting them on top - you'd end up needing to cast your own wheels, but it's really not that hard.

That's in my plans for the next major revision after i've done some testing with this general configuration

i think the motor mounts are also probably of questionable value.

and i need an good way to get access to the electronics without removing the whole top or bottom. (just design notes)
Yeah, I'm not sure I see quite how those work. If they're just angled brackets screwed in from the top, you'll have an interesting time with the torque and G loads.

the idea was they'll fail instead of other parts breaking or bending in awkward to get to places

and also reducing the machining costs for the holes in the uhmw.  and i don't have a bench press.  But I can probably get those holes drilled easily enough.
Yeah... That wouldn't be the easiest thing to do without a bench press, but it'd be doable.
i could add a flat section for a jig. that'd work.
(I'm happy to talk about how I approached these problems with my melty! But this is your channel, I don't mean to intrude.)
I'm interested.

On mine, I built separate sub-frames for the drive assemblies, and then bolted them in as a unit:

At antweight, at least, I've been able to get away with just 3d printing the subframe pieces.
so you have support on both sides of the wheel?

Yup- the motor on one side and a big old bearing on the other.

And the idea with the subframe is that it provides a nice single unit that keeps the axis aligned.

(The subframe as designed doesn't do a super good job of that, being in two different pieces, but it's worked well enough that I haven't iterated on it yet.)

support on both sides has been suggested before. it's going to happen probably, depending on motor choices. especially if I decide to go ridiculous.... it'll probably also support the shaft better so it won't break as easily.
my previous builds have all been aluminum chassis based. this is going to be my first one with an AR500 core and plastic body. working out how to do fastening so the layers don't all just come apart the first time I hit something. I've got a bunch of dowel pins in the frame, and hope that's all I need. ;/ 
but also I think I can just bolt directly onto UHMW so that's probably how I'm going to roll for this version.
Yup- UHMW gets along well with plastic screws.

[just for fun one - could be either meltybrain or flamethrower called "a real meltybrain". playing in fusion while I think.]

the current new round one has double the MoI around the "round-and-round" axis than the crayon shaped one did.  not going back. ;]

cool idea probably leading nowhere: if you detect you are suddenly moving in the opposite direction, change motor direction (if you have a symmetrical weapon like me) 
like, if you were spinning and hit something and now are spinning the other way, don't fight it. that means we did good momentum transfer and we should use it... but could be tricky if we're not on the ground; can probably use the gravity calibration vector to check for that part. 
(if we're in freefall, perhaps turn off the motors to reduce the chance of spin flipping into a wall?)
Or turn on the motors to gyro yourself into the right position

with two motors it's still only going to get one degree of freedom coz they're aligned through center of mass, so it could only affect that component of rotation.  maybe if i had 3 wheels. (but then extra friction, unless one wheel was just a reaction wheel mounted vertically)
a vertical reaction wheel could potentially help/do all the translating too.

would need to mount the wheels further apart or put the reaction wheel off axis. way too early for me to think how that affects things. moving a spinning thing within a spinning frame.where's my rotating grid paper so I can sketch this. ;/

i guess i could do asymmetric adjustment...  would need multiple phases of killing rotation. still like the reaction wheel idea, and would work with 1 wheel melty too.

(reaction wheel + one drive wheel I thought should be called a "gyro melty". and i imagine it's made of sliced meat and wrapped in a pita with cheese on top. )
I had gyros for dinner last night, this is menu item and it's called a Skepasti 😄
nice. when I get around to making one I'll put that as the type ;]

FWIW, in my year of running melties, I've never flipped over from being tossed while spinning - the gyroscopic forces from just the spinning of the melty itself mean that it goes straight up and comes straight back down. You've already got an invertible design, too, so I don't expect being flipped will be too much of a problem for you?

only if i forget to put the lights on the bottom after testing

but yeah it's probably fine while spinning. the gyro thoughts were  mostly because of when I've been flipped while not spinning.  but if i'm not spinning i've probably got other issues.

Yeah... It does happen, but it's not good.

(I've been flipped while not spinning a total of twice.)

> not spinning
well there's your problem
I was flipped once while spinning, but that was back when I had the more rectangular design.  I haven't flipped once since I went with the circular melty.
yeah, the box shape is much worse at spinning flat than the circular one. I imagine the crayon/bar shaped design would be at least as bad.

last "good" brain board.

the latest got so simplified i'm just going to solder it on a perf board.

moved all the pins i'm using to the right side of the teensy so really all I'm going to do is a little power board so I can plug in a BEC and get the 5v i need for the Teensy, the LEDs, and the receiver. I think I can use a digital pin for the accelerometer power.

then I'll have to design a little 3d printed thing to put all the bits in.
Ooh, very shiny.

need check how the WS2812B looks at high speed. ~10 LEDs from center to edge, ~20 for edge-to-edge.  specification says it can do at least 400Hz which is probably good enough. that should also remove two pins (LED_*  => LED_PIN)
Does that put the accelerometer in the way of the Teensy's USB port?

yeah. it was a redesign of a redesign, and unfortunately the last one which designed that worked well enough. I rotated the teensy to make some routing easier without looking at the 3d model. ;/ means I can't debug it with that board while testing

i ended up putting the accelerometer pins on the other side with some boards

but those ones failed during use

(meant i needed to solder the accelerometer upside down too)

i already soldered one board wrong with the current design. that's is what happens when you decide you don't need to label which side is front, but also label almost everything on both sides.
The accelerometer goes on the back of this board. Both are only soldered from one side. 

Eventually I want to put an STM32 on a board, but I'm bad enough at design that my last attempt at a controller board failed. 
If I can make something with exactly the same footprint as the accelerometer (using Sparkfun H3LIS331 board)  that would be awesome.  or if I could find a chip at least as good as the ADXL375 that didn't require JLCPCB "Standard" assembly I could make one really tiny.
Ye- I've got some H3LIS331DLs in-stock at JLCPCB, I'm happy to free them up for cost.
that is the board i just received today. (not assembled)

(Which is still not inexpensive, they're something like $16 apiece)

Ooh, shiny board

that should keep everything together nicer than what I'm going to bodge together later. this will be bigger than that, but i've got months to iterate.

https://www.sparkfun.com/products/14480 i decided not to make my own HLIS331DL accelerometer board when i saw the price of these ones.

which is why that's my planned maximum footprint for an STM32 board.

Ooh, that is a very good rate.

Good luck getting a STM32 that small!

yeah, I expect it's going to be like 1/2 again for headers

if i get the layout just right with the right chip i think I can do all the pins I need. but the teensy spoils you with so many serial ports.

I hope the next version will be able to use TX5 to control the neopixels in a similar way to how the teensy dshot library works.  (https://github.com/DamarXCV/Teensy-DShot)
that would mean I could take the bottom three pins off and just use one to replace them.

it looks like I can just keep writing garbage to mythical devices off the end to swallow up garbage when you're doing other things. so maybe use the serial port's.
0: 250  1000    1250            1: 750  500     1250    --      0.2000:0.8000   0.6000:0.4000
0: 500  750     1250            1: 750  500     1250    --      0.4000:0.6000   0.6000:0.4000

https://wp.josh.com/2014/05/13/ws2812-neopixels-are-not-so-finicky-once-you-get-to-know-them/ <= based on timing ranges in this post

I personally ended up using APA102C based LEDs. They have clock and data lines and can update at a much faster rate than the WS2812B chips. 
If you want to have animation, text, etc shown on the bot (meaning dozens of updates per revolution) the WS based ones may be too slow

I think at the moment I'm just wanting to update 10-20 pixels at a few hundred hertz. any info i could read on it as it spun would be chunky anyway. if I can get it updating using serial, it won't be using the delay loops. it's apparently quite sloppy in what it accepts, so even though this wouldn't be fast, it would be simple. (10 pixels @ 400Hz would be plenty.)

Sounds good
Main thing I was running into was that even at 2500 RPM, that's ~40 rotations per second, which means a max of 10 updates per rotation.

It's only for tracking (on the edges) and displaying the current accelerometer value (from the center and out)... and probably other visual debugging things.  10 updates per rotation is usable.
if it's too slow, I'll drive slower and upgrade the LEDs later.

https://wp.josh.com/2014/09/03/inside-neouart-tricking-a-serial-port-into-being-a-signal-generator/ <= looks like it's been done
DId I understand correctly, you want to power the accel with the MCU IO?

i wanna look at it. see if it works. if it doesn't, then I won't use it. if it does then I could just connect 4 pins in a row on the teensy instead of needing 4 extra connectors.  I've use it for other little boards before  turning them on and off as they were needed.

should be fine...
Well it will work, but I would refrain from doing so. Like is it that hard to have one trace leading to the VCC pin?

i want to get rid of the board

the teensy's normal pins can drive ~20mA iirc (edit: oops. it's complicated,  but somewhere between 1 and 5mA at 100% duty, and more at lower?). so I could move my LED pins down two pins so there is a gap below the SCL and SDA pins. That would let me use pin 16 for ground and pin 17 for 3v3 for the accelerometer. (This would have the side effect of making the board's LED blink when the red LED is on. i could move one of them to pin 20 but i want to save that for telemetry. and if the neopixel stuff works, I don't have to worry about moving anything, just removie two LED pins.)

could put a female header on the i2c side of the accelerometer so it goes under the teensy. would be very low footprint.

(could solder them together for even smaller footprint)

hmm. i would need more space around the board. I could cut the corners off near the pins and file them flat coz there's no traces around them.

the pins on the other side of the board are all disconnected.... i could cut the holes off of that side.

in order to keep the board working, there would still be an obstruction one pin up; this is where the teensy's pins and that board would overlap. I could minimize the number of pins on the other side of the teensy that I couldn't access by removing all of the lower corner holes too.

(teensy is 7 pins wide, the accelerometer is 8)

purple area could go. yellow should go? need to be careful around vias. it's probably ok, considering it's not super high speed or anything.

realistically all i'm losing on the left side of the teensy is trivial access to one serial port. if i need one, there's another at the top of that row.

so the bottom ones aren't really helping that much.

It appears to work.

(the missing pins can be filled in, I just don't need 3v3 or the last two on the end unless i can't get the neopixels working.)

obviously needs support of some sort under/around the board.

(won't actually have pins on left side in final version i think)

also will use multi-pin connectors.

major issue with neopixel type things is they are too pretty. I could stare at them for hours while they smoothly blend.
and they use a lot of power. it's pulling 500mA at 5v for 64 pixels (not even going up to max brightness). up to 280mA for 20, which is what i'm thinking of using. not _terrible_ except compared to everything else I've used.

using the Adafruit_Neopixels library I can refresh 20 pixels 1000x a second. I am almost certain it will not play nicely with the melty code as is.
i need to work out how to bring a HadwareSerial line low to trigger the update frames. I suspect that if I send a signal which causes it to be low except for the start bit that I can get it to ignore the start bit. (the length of time it takes for one bit is less than 1/2 of the minimum needed to be detected... it might not be a problem.  but if it is, then I'll read the Teensy library code again, this time to see if there is a "break" like the raspberry pi has.

Are you having logic level issues with the neopixels running at 5V? I did a recent project that had a 3.3v controller and 5v neopixel strip, and it was pretty unstable trying to run animations until I dropped the voltage down a bit on the neopixels. They will happily run undervolted at around 3.7v (but slightly less bright), and then they can accept a 3.3v logic signal from the microcontroller. I just bought some parts to test this out and see if it will work on my melty; gonna try using a Teensy 4.0 and DotStar strip and run them both from a 1S lipo, which I'm hoping removes the need for multiple BECs and extra circuits, at least on the prototype. I'm not at the point of doing custom boards yet.

it's working fine since I gave the 5v and 3v3 a common ground.

I'm trying to migrate the LEDs i was using now to use the WS2812Serial stuff. having some weird reboot issues. I think it's rebooting coz I'm running the Teensy naked, and it wants accelerometer and IBus channels to at least exist on startup. It's not even making it to the accelerometer code.

unfortuantely the successful tests were all powered by clipping the pins into the jaws of the alligator clamps on my power supply. for actual use I still need to solder up something to extend the 5v and gnd pins so I can plug in power to the receiver and the LEDs as well as the teensy.

accelerometer is not being detected, except it did get detected before this. probably need to check the pinMode flags. nope. may have damaged pins? it worked yesterday. ;/ perhaps from desoldering. RIP board.

https://www.amazon.com/EWFcVBR-Brushless-Compatible-Helicopter-Multicopter/dp/B0DKSDWJBM <= maybe? sketchy af.

that's only a few dollars more each than the  D3536 (2814 stator) cheap Amazon motors I'm using now...

the two  motors have have incompatible mounting pattern though so need to decide soon. may have to slightly widen openings, and need  to put the holes in the UHMW for the motor into the files for manufacture.

at >$90 it's too high for a late night impulse purchase.

amazon sucks for searching for motors and stuff. I should probably use somewhere else.

I think those suck. or at least, I don't think they're a good enough risk to buy right this second.

https://www.amazon.com/FLASH-HOBBY-brushless-multirotor-multicopter/dp/B08LN1MJ7X this is maybe better. the same crappy motor maker that I currently use. (coz they were the first ones I got which didn't suck)
These are a little wider, but much shorter than the current motors
Those look a bit more reasonably sized for a beetle, too.

when designing my first robot i looked at the 2836 can and thought "two of those don't look strong enough to move me." and ordered several 35xx motors. of course while waiting for the bigger motors I tested the "tiny" ones and they worked fine.   I think getting the next size up is a bad design habit I've acquired. I sometimes forget I've already upsized something and upsize it again.

but those flat ones should be an improvement. Lighter + stronger.  And they're under my threshold for impulse purchase ;]

hehe... there's _almost_ enough room now to put two motors per wheel ;] I think I'll save the quad motor melty version for a "truly bad ideas" bot.

and I'll probably need to actually design wheels to fit this motor. it does have a nice drill pattern that i think I could mount a hub on (as a first solution))

i might also shrink the radius an inch or two. and see if I can move the motor mounts into the wall. (just the base... don't want any spinning in there.)

hmm. openmelt2 does alternating "translate forward" and "translate backward" cycles to stay still. that would, strangely, probably fix part of my "the direction indicator changes when I accelerate" issue.
instead of just being in "spin on the spot" mode when not translating, giving a constant signal to both motors, the code instead pretends you alternate between wanting to go forward and backwards on each successive rotation. this means the bot is always pulsing its engine, and so maybe won't have as much of a change in acceleration as my code does with the cosine-shaped motor speed modulation. 
i suspect the motors find it much easier to slow down  than speed up, and it's easier to speed up if you are on the side which is going slower (you're affected a lot less by drag in general if you are going slower.) The more powerful motor should be more symmetrical especially with the lighter 2" wheel.

i've tried some brute force shaping functions on the pure cosine shape to try keep the motor spinning at a roughly consistent speed. but it didn't work well.. 
the more time that the bot is translating, the slower it will get because it isn't restoring all the speed it had at the beginning.   This is non-linear. ;/ 
the bigger motor will (hopefully) allow better translation.

Mount webcam in test box? try taking maximum value of last 4 or 5 frames to try capture and highlight LED streaks to mimic persistence of vision?

options for improving aim: 
- implement and tune PID controller (without exploding this time)
- change relative phase of modulation between each wheel from 180 degrees to anything  between 0 and 180 degrees.
  - both wheels will modulate the speed relative to the phase over every rotation
  - currently m1 and m2 are:
   - 180 degrees out of phase (ignoring shaping)
    - speeding up on one side when the other is slowing down.
  - idea is to have a constant modulation centered on wherever you are "pointing".
    - to spin in place both motors will be in phase.  The modulation will not have any effect on the position as the velocities of both wheels add to 0.
    - to translate at full speed, the motors will be 180 degrees out of phase. The modulation will tend to cause translation in the desired direction as there will be an imbalanced force in that direction.
    - to translate at an intermediate speed the relative phase will be moved to an intermediate angle, with the target angle between both phases.
For spinning in place I just use the same throttle value for both motors, no modulation at all.

So do I, but when I move I modulate, and it slows down... and the changing forces screws with the direction indicator. I want to give it a constant force.

and smoothly change how it is applied
Do you have esc braking enabled?  Some people have mixed results with it, but it made translation smoother for me.
I've had conflicting advice on braking. i'll do some experimenting early next year. i think i'm using very light braking.

Yeah, some people love it, some hate it. It worked for me.

I also limited the amplitude of the waves to 40% of the requested throttle value.  That was somewhat arbitrary, but limits how much the motors have to change in a cycle.

i use a shaped curve maxing at 25%, also arbitrary.  if you oversaturate  the throttle it changes the percentage.

i  guess I should re-implement the  PID controller for rotation speed.

It hasn't been an issue yet, but I also run with a software imposed limit of 60% throttle unless I hit an override switch. The throttle was super twitchy otherwise, and maxed out the accelerometer well before max throttle.

Until I make my next iteration I'm limiting myself to about 40% so I don't throw anymore wheels.  The "box with wheels bolted on" design is terrible in so many ways. ;/

I had quadradic shaping of my throttle so the low end is actually usable, but that causes the top 10% of the throttle to map to like the top 40% of the range. super twitchy... i was thinking I probably only need "fast enough to move,  fast enough to move well, and ramming speed (which would be 100 then down a bit)" . I've disabled that part of it i think while I change everything else about my code.

I'm debating whether I should get my high speed camera out this week to see if I can tell how much authority the motors have over the wheels. 
it would be fun, and it might give me useful information.
it means I also need to set up multiple lights and walk around everything until I pack it up again. ;/  it can wait.

yeah, I've got two bots to build before II get that out. so mid-January or early Feb.

different weapon. internals showing battery wire channel. (top 2 layers on top down are hidden. Top CF/Top UHMW]/AR-500 + Center UHMW/Bottom UHMW/Bottom CF )
4mm holes through UHMW and AR-500 are for 4mm steel dowel pins. 3mm holes through everything are for M3 bolts. (yellow box is battery for CoM calculation)
need wire routing holes for motor wires.
need actual way to mount wheels.
plates for mounting motors or just flatten side? cannot mount with four bolts in any other orientation due to 3/8" AR-500 plate. ok to be that close to edge?

- now you can see the arena floor through the arena floor.  ( + screen cap of a drawing of what it sort of looks like to me. ;] )
- added more bolts for internal walls, as well as access to motor wiring .
- added power switch and access.
- added access hole for neopixels.
- added access panel. 
 - still needs an attachment method. 
 - strain relief method for neopixels?

CoM is a couple of mm off, but I have > 150g to play with.

how important is centering CoM for a melty?

cause it'll spin about the CoM wherever it may be
Yeah, but- if the wheels aren't in line with the CoM, they'll be scrubbing and slipping more.

yea there'd be losses

but i'd expect it doesnt have to be as perfectly centered as a normal weapon would
Traction on a melty is already pretty marginal, I'd lean towards avoiding tempting fate.

I try keep it as balanced as possible. it should at least stand on two wheels and not tilt over... so within a 5-10g

just so i know my accelerometer defaults are close

obviously i can move the battery around or duct tape some washers to the inside if my design is too far out.

you don't need to be perfectly centered, but it keeps the wear-rate down if the CoR of the wheels match the CoM of the robot from wasted friction.

and more traction == more good

also I like the dot being perfectly in the middle ;]

oh yes there is that lol

funny dot must be centered or else

experiment seeing how little effort it takes to make a render look believable.
- Took a picture of my desk and opened it in The GIMP.
- opened the render tab (optional: actually render it and get a transparent PNG instead of this) and then screen shot my robot. (The render tab looks slightly better than the design tab in Fusion). 
- pasted the image into The GIMP and removed the background gray areas.
- duplicated the pasted layer and renamed it (by double clicking on the layer name) "shadow".
- activated the shadow layer selected all and opened  the Color/Threshold tool. i clicked someone in the histogram so it all went black. you could also use the levels tool and move the white color down to 0. You should now have a black outline of your robot.
- Gaussian blur it with a size of about 10-15.
- move the shadow layer below the pasted layer, but keep that layer active.
- select the + shaped arrow Move tool and select move the active layer in the tool options.
- click on the image and drag downward to move the shadow out from under the image until it is in a similar place to other objects.
- adjust the opacity of the shadow layer to make the shadow about the same darkness as the other shadows.

Done.

(i turned off ground plane in the render setup because it always makes the robot float)

the scale is wrong but can fix that with zooming

Not bad! I think the screen shot vs actual render might be what's causing all that aliasing, you could try rendering it at a higher resolution and then scaling it down in GIMP so it doesn't have all the jaggies on the edges

Also if you're cutting the screenshot background, you can do the inside of your wheel wells too to help with the effect. Even though it's a very small part of the image, it helps a lot in selling it

yeah. I was just noticing the edges.

with a render it'd have the transparency

could also set the background to an easier to chroma color than gray.

I don't remember what effects/filter options are available in GIMP, but if you can generate noise patterns, I would also overlay one at very low opacity over just the render layer to break up some of the less realistic textures coming out of Fusion

Specifically the weapon ring and wheel rubber are the two textures that stand out to me

(Sorry, I work in VFX 😅)
Can you take a photo of your desk and use it as the background for the render in Fusion?
That doesn't let you add the shadows as easily

Don't apologize. ;] I learned everything I know about compositing from watching "Corridor Crew" videos. I obviously need to watch more ;]

Slightly better. softened the edges a little, but the green is sneaking in. it's getting a little far from my quick and dirty approach, but still only a few minutes.

the higher resolution is probably the important thing for the jaggies. i need to get it oversampling which the screenshot can't do. (added noise.)

(at 50% it looks ok ;] )

testing if my code can work on a XIAO ESP32C3 
that'll pretty much make the bottleneck for shrinking things the connectors. I feel more  ~~expensive soldering  mistakes~~learning experiences coming soon.

https://www.amazon.com/Seeed-Studio-XIAO-ESP32C3-Microcontroller/dp/B0DGX3LSC7 <= these

I saw them in a youtube video about making very light fast and simple racing cars for a plastic track. 
they're they same width as the teensy but shorter. they also have way fewer pins and only one hardware serial port. but network.

started porting. don't expect to get it finished before I leave for holidays.

how many pins do you need for a melty, right?
2-5 for the accel
1 for the heading LED
1-2 for the ESCs
1-2 for the RX

ideally i'd have a few more serial ports.

from 6 on the teensy + USB to 1 + USB is quite an Ooomph.

means i'd only be able to control one esc with dshot at most, but nowhere near needing that kind of speed yet on my test platform. 400Hz PWMis enough right now.

and having network capability should make up for some of the pins for debugging.

nice. i have apparently have 2 hardware serial ports. one just has undefined ports until I set them.
so that gives me two dshot if i need it. i don't think neopixels even use serial on esp32.

> Sketch uses 334836 bytes (25%) of program storage space. Maximum is 1310720 bytes.
> Global variables use 13364 bytes (4%) of dynamic memory, leaving 314316 bytes for local variables. Maximum is 327680 bytes.

just got it compiling.  Trying to keep it working with both Teensy and ESP32 at the moment.
mostly changing interrupt stuff and changing to FastLED library on ESP32 device. 
had to fix IBus again. The ESP32 libraries have changed their Arduino library interrupt syntax since this was written.

TIL C++20 deprecated ++ on volatiles.

I use an RP2040 Xiao on my bot

I use an i2c PWM driver as IO expansion to control the motor drivers

What,  that's annoying. Wonder why.

I could see ++var not working,  because that's increment before use,  but var++ should be fine.
it's something to do with it not being guaranteed atomic apparently.

randomly thinking about how to optimize my impactor when i was visualizing the ring with the impactor sticking out 2 units with width 1 and counterweight opposite that siting out 1 unit with width around 3 units. that gives at most 1 unit of overlap in your bite calculation...
and wondering if it really matters if i'm halving my bite. bite is already reduced by speed.  I think any divide by speed is countered by the square of speed factor in the KE, still leaving a linear increase. 
and if you replace the 3x1 counterweight with a 1x2 impactor, you also get another 1x1 unit to share between the two before you reach the same mass you had with the counterweight. 
the only problem i think i could have is mistaking the front and back while I'm not moving and the lights aren't blinking. if that's where I am, I'll have bigger issues.

  - the Teensy is super overpowered for this.
    - all it's missing is wireless comms - but there are boards for that.
  - Teensy still isn't EOL.
- use two microcontrollers: this is the worst idea?
  - xiao does neopixel and comms, teensy does everything else
     - if it's only doing two things, the xiao should be fine
     - the Teensy can do NeoPixel fine though.
       - but not telemetry: not much non-volatile storage on teensy, plenty on xiao
       - or comms. even if we don't use this ^^^^
  - xiao only does comms: 
    - same as above but we don't question the neopixel choice ;]

the Xiao is basicially the right form factor [it's the same width as a Teeny (7 pins across), but only 7 pins long vs 14. Unfortunately the ESP32 doesn't like doing all the work I want it too with interrupts.

what would be the optimal shape to make a non-planar ring? circular, or pre-distorted so it has a circular profile around the eventual axis of rotation? (I'm thinking like about 5 degrees tilt, not 45. enough to give like 10-15mm of rise across the whole ring.)

Is there a particular reason you want to pre-distort the ring? If the attachments are balanced, it will distort itself into a ring; or in the case of already being a ring, distorting into a slightly larger ring.

You don't need a fake teensy, you just need to be careful and make sure you can't plug the teensy in backwards/offset

it was working and plugged in right on the desktop last night, so there is a short somewhere to high power when the lid was put on. The lights only turn on when the component is plugged in correctly coz they are Neopixels. The intent for a fake teensy is to do the power distribution and enough to activate the device (even Neopixels can be faked to activate some pixels sometimes by tapping the data pin to either ground or 5v) and to tell if things are getting current when they shouldn't. (getting 13v on a data pin probably isn't good. 

it also appeared to turn on momentarily with the power switch off. this also points to a short

bench testing it. (later.)

If it only does it with the top plate on and turns on sometimes with the power off, any chance you're shorting to the CF plates? That's how I found out they were conductive and blew up a receiver and ESC. Now I print a thin TPU layer and glue it to the inside of my CF plates as an insulator.

that was my assumption. the CF is the problem.
Looks like the power switch was shorting to the CF it was pressing right up close to. (and there was also a short from the CF to the bottom of the brain board. redesigning brain box.)

I should know better than this... my previous robots had aluminum bodies.

CF is also only intermittently conductive ;/

I'd already printed some super thin transparent tpu disks the size of the CF to try as a diffuser. I'll just put it on the other side.

if that's not the problem, then I have some other major issues.

i've also shrink wrapped the length of my switch tool so it doesn't short between the switch and the CF

and moved the switch down so it can't physically touch the CF (yay for duplicate redundant mounting holes)

I go through a lot them. ;] (and usually don't order just one)

(didn't see any evidence the tool was shorting it, but with where I moved the switch, I didn't wanna risk it. i've seen it before though on my other robot. usually just before I ordered replacements.)

every time I have an incident like this, I try to step back for a while and not immediately jump into fixing it so I can actually plan things through. (after making the thing safe, of course.)
this is to prevent me implementing "solutions" like just replacing the controller (which would probably just fry immediately again or not long after), and also to give me time to calm down a little.

LED strip for different error codes?

yep. also for visualizing forces (eventually).

i have a virtual 2-d array of pixels and will use the estimated rotation to select the column I want to write pixels to. the idea is I'll add the current accelerometer magnitude to a pixel at a the angle the force was applied, and then fade all the values over time, so it should show the forces affecting you over each cycle, and you'd see what was moving you. (I've already written the code, but it's commented out  while troubleshooting the motor)

interesting - lights farther out when closer to max speed?

Kind-of like how project liftoff "locks in" its LEDs once up to speed

so the outer 4 LEDs will all be either green or off to tell me where the front is, but the other ones are intended to give me a kind of "you're being pushed forward, from the right edge" information.... (taking the accelerometer data and converting it into angles and magnitudes, and then projecting it on the rest of the screen)
because there are generally no net forces acting on you once you're up to speed, it would only show things when you either move in a direction (when it should show something representing a force in the direction you're driving, like a cosine plotted around the robot, because of the way the motors accelerate over a revolution) or when you get hit (when it should show a bright blip which fades over time, showing the extra force that was added))

sorta like radar, but for forces

(could also attach it to distance sensors or whatever in the future for visualizing data as it moved)

because you're already spinning, that gives you an extra dimension, like the "persistence of vision" toys, and assuming you have LED lock it's not _that_ much harder than blinking in time with the rotation.

Robot testing successful!
Wasn't a disconnected wire, was an unplugged cable. fixed it by.... adding more cables. 
- added extension servo cables from the ESCs and the BEC to the brainbox. This gives me more flexibility in routing the cables, and also makes the power header 3pin so it doesn't get misaligned.
- need to increase size of opening for the servo connectors
   - not quite large enough to slide them all in at once: 
      - put all but power cables in opening
      - squeeze the power one in
      - insert the brain board into brainbox.
  - only one servo connector opening,  merging with LEDs on next board
    -  LEDS will be S/+/- like everything else
       - current +/S/-  on board matches actual wire order [red, white, black wires  vs white, red, black  (left->right when looking at back of the strip)
       - requires special cross over splitter, but next board will be able to use std Y shaped splitter.

hmm. I could make the curve on the outside concave.
- with current convex body, any impact moving inwards which hits the surface will  be deflected away from the surface. (as you move in, gradient decreases, becoming flat in the middle. 
  - glancing blow is deflected away from body
- straight line body (wedge/ramp) would just slide impact along body
  - glancing blow is scraped along body
- concave body would deflect inward impact into next part of body... doesn't look good.
  - glancing blow is deflected into the body. ;/

do I need bigger ESCs for the D4215 to get more speed out of it?  Here's the evidence collected over the last couple of days. [this wasn't written down, so is based on my fallible memory]
- The acceleration curve for the D3530 1450kv is very different to the D4215 650kv.  The D4215 flattens off early, while the D3530 keeps on speeding up.
  - when bench testing without motors the throttle curve being sent to the ESC shows a linear mapping from [-1..1] => [1000us..2000us]. 
    - There are no steps and no curves.
    - i.e. when I go to 50%, the ESC is telling the motor to go at "50% power". 
      - It doesn't shape it so it's actually doing 100% at 50% or something. 
      - It's perfectly linear. 
      - No bugs found.
    - Oscilloscope showed expected output on pins connected to the ESCs.
      - PWM waveform that changed smoothly with throttle.
      - this means it was not a code problem or signal problem from the controller to the ESC.
      -  testing without motors was done with battery disconnected. next tests were done with battery connected.

- testing with D4215
    - bench: accelerates quickly from stopped, but at about 50% throttle the speed stops increasing. [initially thought this was a software bug again]
      - maybe could've tried using my bench power supply for higher voltages... probably bad idea. it's not very good and
    - test box: accelerates from stand still to a few hundred RPM quickly, but throttle after about 35% makes no difference to maximum rotation speed. [different max speed when there is resistance on the motors vs none on bench]
  - testing with D3530
     - bench:  accelerates quickly from stopped smoothly from 0% to 100%. Pitch was way higher than D4215 at 100%. 
     - test box:  accelerated to about 30%. got direction lock (similar speed and behavior to previous version of *the arena floor*)
      - previous version had larger wheels but smaller battery. (2-7/8" x 3S vs 2" x 4S... decrease wheel radius by ~30% but increases voltage by ~30%, so I expect similar performance, which it had)

  - the D4215 documentation points at wanting more current. It's capable of drawing 900W @ 24V which is 37.5A ... close enough to 35A. Except at 12v (3S-ish) that'd be 75A. 

the D4215 might just want a bigger ESC (definitely wants a bigger battery... 90C still only gives me 67.5A draw from a 750mAh battery).

I think I've identified the root cause the "not getting lock".
>     if (rps < RPS_THRESHOLD) {
>       rps = RPS_THRESHOLD;
> ...
and rps is calculated using both the accelerometer reading and the radius potentiometer value on the transmitter.
I think the interaction with the radius and that line made it impossible to get lock unless you were already spinning fast enough. 
So I could get 1/2 speed and 1/3 speed locks, but not full speed. (when I tried with higher throttle, it would be even more erratic)

pro-tip: make your important circle centers locked in your sketchs in Fusion. You don't want the solver moving the outer edge across a bit. ;/
Pro tip, make as many independent sketches reference to the assembly XYZ origin instead of random edges of extrusions halfway down your modeling tree, and fully constrain your sketches!!!

that's how I fixed the circles which had slid.

fully constrained is annoying. but ideal. I usually have one real sketch per "part" and make layers which project from it for the variants at different heights. like the three sections in the tpu mount: the part the weapon attaches to, the part where the wires go through it, and the rest of the parts without those gaps. and I keep the stuff that's important (coz I can't change it anymore because it's real and moderately expensive in time or money) constrained so I don't move them around. Each of those features is on the base level sketch for that part, which is like the first non-inserted object in the project timeline usually, The other layers are because it helps when extruding the sections.

You're basically describing master sketch modeling fwiw

- displaying two numbers in different colors, but actually working. 
- and there is a 50:50 chance it will display backwards depending on your spin direction. I could make it so if you press the three way switch to the bottom it reverses the display direction, but just switch motor directions on both sides to reverse it.

The bug was that when I changed it from needing to do a complete loop from beginning to end within the handleMeltybrainDrive(), I had to move the "calculateRPS()" call, which calls the accelerometer from outside the loop to somewhere that restricted how often it could get new info without slowing down anything else. you've mentioned a future code refactor, when you do I recommend looking at the TaskScheduler library; it'll handle all the timing and looping, so you can have an accelerometer read task that sits there constantly updating a global variable that all the other code reads from whenever it's required, and you can schedule the LED task to synchronise with the rotation

the less your functions do the easier it is to debug, and you usually end up with all the main logic in one small function or state machine and the rest is just pulling in pre-processed data from the various sources

if you want to get really fancy you can encapsulate it in classes; I do this because I only have so much space in my head and once a class is finished I can forget about it and focus on the rest of the code

not necessary however

oh boy

GLHF

you can also run two schedulers with high and low priority, so it can prioritise motion functions over debug or logging or whatever that you care about less

Picture shows one potential layout for just headers without the Xiao headers added.
- I2C0 is for high range accelerometer. 
- I2C1 is for low range accelerometer or magnetometer or light sensor or whatever.
- Support is just support for the accelerometer. it only uses the pins on one side for signals/power.
- T-Right is where the Teensy does all the power and signal stuff. T-Left is just there to keep it level.
- The rest are for servo connectors for the ESCs, radio, LEDs, telemetry/automation, and power.

#Bill of Materials
*the arena floor* is a 3lb meltybrain. this version is a redesign sharing only the motor, ESCs, and type of controller board with the original.

This is what is is made of.

Topless picture.

I aspire to better wiring.

it is considerably more hardened than it was on Wednesday night. The battery holder used to be about 2mm all around, and now it's over 6mm. The battery used to be able to move over 15mm. I think it's about 6 or 7mm now. so it moves less and stops faster with less energy going sideways into the switch. (I had the bot stop after one test with a battery connector cable disconnected from its XT-30 connector because the battery had pulled on it at an angle. live battery wires potentially bypassing the switch isn't good. or shorting stuff. or making it not work by breaking. 

what am I doing wrong though? 

my design philosophy was "try to keep each component in its own area". the only exception was the LED cable which currently is really long so I can flip the top plate over and still have it both connected and out of the way during testing; it's helpful to see the pattern of blinking lights sometimes.
the top area is power (except the BEC is down by the brains in the lower right...  so technically it's just the switch and the battery. but conceptually, it would be all the power related stuff (except the stuff the things do themselves. pretend the BEC is on the brain board.)
the wheels and motors are the drive (duh).
now it gets a little tricky: it's 3 sections.
- ESC for motor 1 and associated wiring
- the brain box
- ESC for motor 2 and associated wiring

the "exposed metal" on the lower wire in the power section is the XT-30 i was talkng about, and it actually has a clear heat shrink over it; it's not exposed.

the receiver balances the BEC. that's my excuse for why it's smooshed into the wiring on that side.

i'm also wondering how much of the lock instability I've been having is rigidity based.
the real thing has an AR-500 ring, but this test one is made of (nearly invisible in the picture) transparent TPU. It weighs about 15x less than the real thing and is probably 50x less rigid. The other TPU of the body would have a lot more support.
Do a no impactor ring in PLA to test if the rigidity helps lock better?

currently not. I was using D4215 motors but the facemount patterns on the D3530 has a different size which is slightly larger than the hub radius for the wheel I am using, so it's just the stock hubs.
Not having any slippage issues yet. The issues I have had with these hubs was when they were facing outwards: at about 100g the grubscrew holding the hub on would fail. (at somewhat lower speeds, if I hadn't seated the snap ring properly, the wheel would fly off too but that's just me being stupid.) Because they're under compressive load now they won't fly off. 
I don't think I'll have to worry about the wheels slipping at the hub/shaft interface until I upgrade the batteries again. 
If I do, I'll design and print some abomination of a hub that uses the snap ring but also bolts onto the can.

it'll probably be in two parts, and have captive nuts to hold them together. bolt one part on the can, insert the other part through the wheel and bolt them together.

How do you know what to map though? A small refresh rate just means that you only can tell if something is close, but not what that object is.

Math incoming (I've done this before, just have to dig it up)

1500 RPM = 25 Hz rotation rate.
The VL53L1X (one of the fastest small lidars) has a 50 Hz update rate, meaning, you can get 2 samples per rotation, at approximately 180 degrees from each other.
You can vary your speed, but keeping the speed consistent (or determining angle extremely precisely) is required to get a full "Sweep" of the arena.

It only gets worse if you go faster. 3k RPM is only 1 sample per sweep.

So, every rotation, you'll get 2 distances. How can you tell if that distance is a wall, or a robot?

I think the idea is to spin around like 10 times (0.4 s) and then use all of the samples you've accumulated to map the environment and update movement

Cause you're spinning fast enough that you can spin several times between direction updates
If you're at NHRL how would you differentiate a house bot from a big opponent?

I haven't thought that far

Idk

If you do that though, you have to accurately track your rotation across multiple sweeps. (Otherwise, data points might overlap or not be accurately positioned)
If you sweep once per revolution, you only need to be accurate to one rotation, so your error tolerance is much higher. (driving +- 15 degrees at an opponent is still better than not being able to track them at all)

Yeah it's a trade-off

Too few rotations means you don't get enough samples to get a good view of the field

Too many means that there's more error in the individual measurements
you need a direction reference though, otherwise you can't combine multiple measurements
And a very accurate one at taht

other dumb ideas:
- audio based - basically looking for a low frequency oscillation or repeating pattern... probably not the best approach as another thing spinning might make a similar sound?
- video based
  - can you use rolling shutter to improve temporal resolution from video? 
    - I'm assuming i'll only care about large bands of dark and light changing.
    - lowest resolution, highest frame rate setting
    - ideally the camera will be capturing all the time, but there will likely be a gap between frames. but assuming it takes 1/100th of a second to scan the frame and it's taking pictures every 1/60th of a second, if you're looking at roughly the same vertical angle for each pixel you go down then you'll be converting the vertical direction to the time direction, and it'll be covering 2/3rds of the time from the start of the frame to the start of the next one. 
    - NHRL have rules which give weight exemptions for active recording equipment, as long as it's not an egregious attempt to cram more weight on. 
      - the idea I originally proposed was going to be like 16 cameras with global shutters (which would've been super expensive especially as the actual idea was "free ballast!") that trigger sequentially and send back the data to goggles which I would ignore. 
      - this time the idea is to actually try process the data for rotation rate/direction estimation. (they explicitly exclude this from the "autonomous" features with "Systems such as stability control and meltybrain directional process do not qualify. ")
        - need to get a cheap action cam to see how bad it will be.

At 2k RPM, you're doing ~34 RPS, which means from a 60 FPS camera you get two frames per revolution. I'm not sure this will give you any useful amount of data, assuming you can even process a frame fast enough. Add more cameras and you just need a lot more processing power. Granted it's not the most efficient thing in the world, but when I was processing Twitch streams on a RaspPi 4 and using OpenCV for template matching, I was only grabbing every 4th or 5th frame, scaling them down to 1/2 rez, and then running the template match code to be able to mostly keep up to real time, and that's with multiple cores running a process pool of tasks grabbing a frame off the video stream, scaling it, and the template matching it.

You'd have better luck with a bright LED flashlight as a beacon and a photoresistor or something probably, and hope you don't fight outdoors
I'm thinking turning the image into a 1x240 or however tall image, and using the rolling shutter so the as it rotates it moves down the frame to improve the rate
And at that point you can go back to IR to limit interference

then it's just 1d signal processing

The BPW77N has a narrower field of view: 10 degrees instead of 15 degrees (this is a VERY good thing).
The BPW77N can dissipate more power (a good thing, likely due to its metal housing).
The BPW77N  has twice the Collector emitter dark current (a bad thing, but this is measured in nano-Amps, so not really a worry).
The BPW77N also suffers from ambient temps affecting the dark current by a factor 10x worse than the BVP11.  But, as before, 10nA instead of 1nA.
The BPW77N is not as sensitive (based on Collector light current)... 
The BVP11 has a wider range and better sensitivity (maybe 10% to 15% better).

The tighter field of view could easily make up for the last two short-comings.

i'm thinking about how to make use of sensors with very limited range and resolution. (what trade offs can we make for range/resolution? more power <= more LEDs, )
only having the close part of your map gives you the local parts but not the overall picture. 
you're only going to be able to distinguish between  corners and walls, and a single wall is going to be most of what you see. if you're not within range, no walls.
corners let you detect objects. corner is two lines intersecting. the more  corners the better, up to like 5 or so i guess. (probably won't have more than two bots visible "in front" of you at a time and even if you did, you wouldn't behave any differently)
could perhaps do a simple "vision" algorithm based on corners, which are in turn based on the slopes of the detected lines to identify whether it's an arena corner, a housebot corner, or a sticky-out-corner of some kind which deserves our attention.
I imagine it would result in the same type of data as the "bot in the middle of one wall" test, except data is only revealed in a direction if there is an object close enough to it. 
[I come to a point in the next blob]

my robot has NeoPixels on the top. i predict if, in time with the rotation, I output the detected signal by illuminating a pixel whose position varies based on value then it will draw a map on the robot.  
if I could scale my robot up to the maximum range without it hitting the walls, I would see the neopixels trace out the shape of the box it was in.
if it was instead less than 2 feet across (but still didn't hit the wall until a normal sized one would hit the wall) then in a 4'x4' box it wouldn't see anything unless there was another robot or a wall nearby. 
it would be like moving revealing only a rectangle of the data from the graph at as you moved around the test box.
when another robot comes into view it would either be a wall moving towards you (does the motion make sense for another robot or could it be we're moving into a wall? ) or a corner moving towards you.
if robot is driving directly at you it would look like a wall (very rectangular box meta assumption: what about Wej??) otherwise like an inward pointing corner (box bias?)

staying away from the wall where you can't see it might give you better vision and allow you to react appropriately to an opponent who wants to ram you [this was supposed to be the point]

that would be separating the mapping functionality from the targetting functionality

Sampling an ADC very fast comes at some cost to the resolution. I am sampling fast enough to get 512 samples if rotating at 4000 RPM.  My range and resolution would likely be better if I did not sample so fast. My Bots at high speed rotate ~3300 rpm, most of the time they are spinning ~2500rpm.  I will slow down the sampling and see if the range improves, maybe I could have that done by mid-April.  I'd like to build an 8x8' test box; then the tests would match the actual conditions.

Yeah, I too have spent (too much) time pondering how to improve the sensor.  ToF sensors have come down in price, but their sampling rate is too low and their pipeline delay are too long.  Very few robotic applications need a sampling rate higher than 1000Hz. I don't believe we'll see a ToF sensor with sampling higher than 1000Hz.

Delay and syncing.  Making sure the delay is deterministic so that the sampling is evenly spaced is tricky

Yeah COTS parts just aren’t made for us. I had struggled finding a magnetometer that would work, none of them would even list the max rotation speed on the datasheet. It wasn’t a consideration any if their target customers cared about.

There was one(1) I could find that had sample rate over 100Hz and let me set the number of sample repetitions

The dirty secret here is most motion sensors repeat the measurement multiple times and average the results. It’s a more cost effective way to improve sensitivity, but it imposes a hard limit on rotation rate for us
https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-22/chapter-I/subchapter-M/part-121
The BMM150 mag, even set to one repetition, still takes on the order of microseconds to take a measurement, ~5deg of travel distance. I’m on the bleeding edge of what’s possible in COTS

it's a fingertech screw switch, but i've drilled it out to 3mm mounting holes. the two 30mm screws i use to hold it in place go around (and actually touch) a steel round nut.

there is an electrical connection between the bolts holding in the switch and the round nut above the the switch.  the weapon would also be connected if it wasn't made of TPU.  there is no connection to this 'circuit' from the power.

there is a connection from the battery to the screw terminal of the switch. this is at the same potential as the battery wire on that side. (0v if you want to call the black wire that) the opposite side is at BAT+ voltage and is not connected unless the switch is closed.

You need coils and a high precision amplifier, along with some fancy current sources for the Hall effect. We tried to roll our own coil magnetometer,  and ended up going COTS instead. 

The earth's magnetic field is just so small that you need to have a lot of gain and filtering on it to get good signals.

Typically It's hall effect for COTS mags

But yeah we did try a coil version. Couldn't get enough sensitivity with PCB coils

ok. i knew antennae could be made and in ICs they make capacitors and transistors so thought maybe it was doable that way. 
so it's just a 'there are too many zeros'  problem

scaling doesn't always happen to work in convenient ranges

Our hand-wound air core coil picked it up fine. And it's not that bad to integrate. But you still get the noise issues I'm dealing with, and you have to do all of the analog front end. It's just way easier to slap down the BMM150 and call it a day

We would resort to it if we didn't have a good COTS sensor

Fair warning, a lot of crushable foams are viscoelastic, i.e. they'll deform differently over time while loaded depending on load and will expand slowly even with load removed, sometimes not to their original position. Creep is also possible/likely and will throw off calibration whenever it happens. There are tricks you can use but you probably want a metal that you can keep in the elastic region.

The usual go-to of strain gauges with a wheatstone bridge circuit is standard for a good reason

so just had an idea: if the system determines you have the throttle up at start up, it'll first perform a random number of random evasive maneuvers by sending random values to the throttles (biased towards actually moving either forwards or backwards with that motor, and not towards staying still) of your drive motors.
the intent is if the system has rebooted mid-fight, the throttle would likely be up. if the the throttle is up at reboot time, we must've crashed, so may be out of control and been that way for an unknown length of time. performing random throttle actions will cause the robot to change its orientation and potentially move forwards or backwards too. this would get you out of the way of anyone currently scurrying towards your previously incapacitated husk.

alternatively, I could just ignore it and pretend nothing happened and let the driver "keep" control. (if it was quick they might not even notice)

## Mass distribution of _the arena floor_ in grams. Bill of Materials thread is here: https://discord.com/channels/426513584428679168/1346331022572261398
### the TPU body takes up the remainder to get the weight up to 1360g. (or within a few grams) 
The undercutter is marginally heavier than the midcutter config.
### per wheel + hub + motor    119.3  *2
### top plate + bottom plate    64.3 *2
### Tatu Rline 750 mAh 95C 4S battery    83.7
### weapon ring    555 (originally)
### Brainbox gross weight    100.7
### battery box    28.6
### fingertech switch + m&f xt30 connectors + some wire    (est)10g?
(it was stuck. i have to take it apart more to get the switch out.)
### for all 8 @ M4x25mm round nuts    15.8g
holds the top and bottom plates together gives something to mount the weapon on.
### 13 @ M4 button head hex drive bolt    15.1
there are 8 on the bottom plate so everything is moderately rigid. i only used 5 on the top coz it's a pain to remove them.
realistically the top and bottom plates are just to stop the insides from falling out. They're probably not much use against an actual weapon.

I think the only difference in BoM is now trying 60A wheels.

this is a screen grab of the weapon geometry from my SCS order.
it came in exactly on weight (after a light flap disk sanding)

the holes are sized to accept the M4x25mm round nuts, and those are embedded in the TPU to distribute forces from the AR500 into the TPU.
This geometry lets the ring diameter be a little larger than the internal space, which is the circle that touches each of the round nuts. 
A larger ring means more MoI.
And a larger ring means deeper protecting armor.

Are you worried about tpu being too flexible for accerometers?

i was, but now I can manually tweak the "RPM vs expected G-force" curve while it's running anyway.  I move the 3-position switch into the middle position, and then it uses the left potentiometer as the radius input. I tweak it until the LEDs appear to  stop rotating, then toggle to the 3rd position and back to the middle and it's saved the new RPM value for the current force, and adds that to the curve. (i can clear the whole curve and start again by holding it in the 3rd position for over 5 seconds. then it will just use the left potentiometer value until I program it.)

so as long as the flexing occurs in the  "outward" direction, it'll compensate with the curve. (it dies a  stepwise linear interpolation of what i've programmed in. )
so i want to run for a minute without saying the values to get it up to normal operating temperature in case that matters, then I'd slow down to like 500 rpm, then 1000, then 1500, then 2000, then in between the values. usually it would be in the places i like the put the throttle.

the TPU will still be supported by the round nuts. 
the only time it might move is when it gets a big impact. and at that point, i want as much flexing as possible.

different kind of vibration
both seem like the type that disconnects plugs from boards

when you do commercial shock and vibration testing they're different tests - impacts are more akin to a drop test, whereas vibration is done on a table that does frequency sweeps in different axes

sustained low displacement vibration is a killer for things like unscrewing screws and rattling wires loose

i'm not saying "if i can survive it, then I can survive being hit." just "if i can't survive it, then i probably have systemic issues"  it should just be shaking it at a continuously variable frequency. and the tpu will tend to conform with the surface if it's too low, dampening the shocks.

Is there any advantage to having your wheels closer vs farther to the center?

the main reason they face that way is so the wheels don't get pulled off at high spin rates. Otherwise it's basically gearing... i can use wheels which spin slower to get higher tip speeds.
I'm using a 400g range accelerometer and at 40mm that maxes out at 3000 RPM. the wheel, when facing outwards at that speed would be about 80mm out is pulling double that. i've had the wheel pulled off the shaft multiple times facing the other way.

instead of being at 80mm from the center, the wheel is at about 50mm, so it's only pulling about 500G, and it's in compression, instead of 800G pulling on the shaft or the can.
Gotcha
it also adds some MoI though, putting the motor mass further out. whether that is good or not depends on how you like hitting harder vs being fast to spin up
Id imagine spinning faster would do more damage in the same setup? And you might get up to speed a little faster as well

the further in the wheels are, the less distance they need to go to make the robot spin around, but the harder it is to spin it.  it's like having a high gear ratio from the motor to the tip.
i'm wanting to use some low KV motors so I have the torque at low speed to get spun up.  If i put the wheels further out I would need to drive faster to reach the same RPM because the circumference increases with the radius. I can use a slower but more powerful motors closer in than I would need if I had the wheels further out.

tip speed is the important speed for doing damage

Gotcha gotcha, what rpm/distance do you typically aim for?

I have no clue what the specs are on my fairy atm(cheap aliexpress motors) but am definitely going to redesign it

with my bots, 2000-3000 RPM is where i'm aiming. I'll typically have the accelerometer much closer to the center of rotation, and the real limits i run into are battery current to get me up near the limits. 
at higher speed translation also gets harder as you don't have as much throttle headroom for the modulation.

you want to be going as fast as you can while still being able to translate at a reasonable speed.

you won't do any damage if you just gently slide up to your opponent. You want to have some closing speed.

Is aiming to pingpong off the walls a legitimate strategy for getting bite?

Mine doesn’t have code atm so I just tank drive forward and spin up before i hit something
I wouldn't count on it, personally. Bounces are fun, but very random
you really need to be moving towards your opponent to do any damage at all... or have them moving towards you.  otherwise it'll just brush your impactor against them and maybe slightly push you away.

For calibration, you're picking a throttle speed, adjusting the distance from CoR (or some offset number) manually until your header LED stops moving, and then saving that offset for that throttle speed, correct?

yeah. i  sample that at several different speeds and generate a curve so I can do a lookup based on acceleration and get an RPS result, from saving the current acceleration and  RPS (calculated from the radius, adjusted on the transmitter) in a table. I do piecewise linear interpolation of the two bounding samples (the one above and the one below my lookup).  the curve should be a parabola sitting at the origin, but it's actually offset from it due to sensor noise.  if the value is past the end, it just uses the last value and doesn't try interpolate.

so you were mostly right, except it's not for the throttle speed, it's for the acceleration. because the throttle vs rotational speed can vary as the battery drains, but acceleration vs rotational speed is constant

that is a super low profile. You are actually maybe immune to big horizontals now

i'm already limited by battery size to at least 28mm thick which is what it currently is. the main thing this design might do is reduce the weight of the TPU.  but i'm not really that weight constrained atm: i've got about 50g of weight tape attached to one side of the bottom plate to balance it, but if i move the battery i wouldn't need that.

i drew a few circles with lines in them to try visualize a good top down  shape. i think i could do 3 sections, with the battery being in the middle section, and covered with like 2mm AR600 plate.  if i make the front and back narrow enough i could do the same for them. otherwise it'll be spring steel and wishful thinking protecting the electronics.

would have to integrate the battery box into the frame though, which I'm not a huge fan of. otherwise i'll have trouble getting past the battery with wires coz it'll fill the whole middle.

the main issue with this if someone is shorter than 20mm or so, my weapon in this config will go right over them.
and the undercutter config can't do the double slope.

actually, that could be a very cool undercutter shape:
it's basically a strip along the middle between the wheels, cut in three and converted to 2mm ar500. the middle section is tall enough for batteries and to mount the wheels so the blade is 3mm from the ground. the corners would be 3d printed only, but also really swept back... possibly just bare weapon ring with an air gap between it and the body in places.

how much I can remove it depends on how much space i need with the new layout.

i _think_ that i could find weight for the 2mm AR600 panels on the top, but the bottom would probably take sacrificing some weapon. panels would weigh about the same as 10mm of CF (assuming 0.4mm per spring steel sheet, and that 2mm CF weighs same as 0.4mm spring steel of same shape, and spring steel is similar in density to AR600) but would be smaller than current panels coz they wouldn't have the corners or the part over the wheels.

coz 2mm is available whereas only 3mm for ar500

and i want something that'll skate most weapons

someone pasted this on another server... i wonder if you can actually put out of range values into a config and get enhanced braking for example.

(they had upgraded to 2.19 i think without resetting the config to defaults. the new version reads the config from uninitialized memory, which is by default filled with 0xff... or 255 in decimal. at least that's how i also had a similar issue with some ESCs)
I expect put of range values will just break things

but maybe they have conservative values for whatever the parameter adjusts by default? maybe putting 20 in would give me double the braking, at the expense of heating up the MOSFETs faster?

they probably don't do range checking because that's all done on the client side of setting the settings. so it would do weird things. if you go putting 255 in it'll probably break things, but maybe 20-50 might work. 
hmm @ the "servo low value", "servo mid value", and "servo high value" parameters. if you squeezed all of you PWM signals into a smaller space, could you update them faster than 400Hz?
i wonder if i could do something like a split cosine function, where if the cosine value is negative, it's used directly, but if it's positive it's added to the current throttle.
You might be able to achieve 'active breaking' by increasing the timing advance by 90 degrees when braking is desired?

i think i could deal with even 4 bit resolution for throttle.  
is serial transmission speed the bottleneck for DSHOT control or is it the actual electronics of the ESCs?
Depends on the ESC
for a meltybrain you don't need the throttle resolution that a rock crawler would want, so maybe there's a better protocol for it?

If youre using dshot there are some predefined but unused command codes

You could write something that uses them https://brushlesswhoop.com/dshot-and-bidirectional-dshot/

you want to slam between forward or zero or reverse as fast as possible

and if you can send 1 byte instead of 2, you can update twice as often
idk if the time to send one frame on dshot (26.72 microseconds on dshot600) is the limiting factor there. the momentum of the motor probably will have a bigger effect on responsiveness

i think i'll try the "force reverse throttle for cos(x) < 0, but add to throttle for > 0" idea.

that's not terrible to code.

and i'll only run it for like 20 seconds to see how it does for heat
Wouldn't you just break traction at that point?

the cos(x) is multiplied by the "stick length" (how far the top of the stick is from the center position).

if it always spins, then it's a bad idea. i think giving it not quite enough power to spin would help

Braking is already much stronger than acceleration, you'll spin down significantly when translating if you use it too aggressively

so I did a bit of thinking in bed last night about how to drive a meltybrain.
when you drive a normal 2wd (or 4wd) and you say "go forward", it translates the robot at a speed proportional to how much forward you push the control stick. i.e. it translates you proportional to input.
my meltybrains feel more like a rocket: it's like you're applying a force to them, not setting a speed.

i wonder if i could somehow do some PID magic on the actual inputs to make it try keep constant velocity (i.e. if you command forward and it detects you're actually accelerating in reverse (i.e. slowing down) then it should increase the throttle until it detects you're not accelerating). it would need to use a very low pass filtered version of the velocity, probably over an entire revolution. hmm.

it would also have to be able to  "if you command forward and you're actually accelerating forward, that's cool and you shouldn't change that until you've been doing that for a while? i don't know maybe integrate it to work out how fast you're going?"
Can I ask why it shoots forward like a rocket? Mine translates at a speed proportional to the stick input (to a point)
Maybe it speeds up due to the uneven response  between acceleration and deceleration and that's giving me the feeling it's accelerating as I move. hmm.

<@707053739432738856> iirc you have used the repeat 35A rated am32 drive escs before - how much did you have to limit power? Were running a similar setup to test before our vortex escs come in

i think i limit to 80A but don't have the robot open at the moment. that was based on guessing the "actual" C rating of the  battery i was using and adding something.
i think you can get away with not limiting them most of the time as long as you're careful about running them stalled... and hopefully you can't actually stall your wheels. 
worst thing that happened to the repeat 35A was a resistor desoldered when the other motor seized and it had to drag the whole robot (and the dead wheel) around on one ESC. It lasted until 20 seconds before the end of the fight. the resistor was standing upright tombstoned on the ESC.

the vortex are nice but a little big for my current designs. you definitely want to limit those to something sensible so they don't destroy themselves.
